content:
Which among the following processes is/are associated with increasing bond order but no change in diamagnetic/paramagnetic behaviour?

(i) $\mathrm{N}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{~N}_2^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-}$

(ii) $\mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_2^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-}$

(iii) $\mathrm{O}_2+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_2^{-}$

The dipole moments of three $\mathrm{AB}_3$-type molecules $\mathbf{I}, \mathbf{I I}$, and $\mathbf{I I I}$ are measured to be $0.0 \mathrm{D}, 0.2 \mathrm{D}$, and 1.5 D , respectively. Which one of the following options is correct regarding the identity of $\mathbf{I}, \mathbf{I I}$, and $\mathbf{I I I}$ ?
Which one of the following statements is correct about $\mathrm{N}_2, \mathrm{CO}$, and $\mathrm{NO}^{+}$?

options.content: These are isoelectronic and have identical bond order.
These are isoelectronic and have different bond orders.
These are not isoelectronic but have identical bond order.
These are neither isoelectronic nor have identical bond order.

explanation:
Isoelectronic Series and Bond Order

Isoelectronic Series

An isoelectronic series refers to a group of molecules or ions that share the same number of electrons. These entities generally possess identical electronic configurations, leading to similar chemical and physical properties.

Bond Order

The bond order of a molecule can be determined using Molecular Orbital Theory. It is calculated with the formula:

$ \text{Bond Order} = \frac{1}{2} (\text{Number of bonding electrons} - \text{Number of antibonding electrons}) $

Application to $\mathrm{N}_2, \mathrm{CO}$, and $\mathrm{NO}^{+}$

1. Molecular Orbital Configurations

Nitrogen ($\mathrm{N}_2$):

$ \left[\left(\sigma_{1 s}^2 \sigma_{1 s}^*{ }^2\right)\left(\sigma_{2 s}^2 \sigma_{2 s}^*\right)\left(\pi_{2 p_x}^2=\pi_{2 p_y}^2\right)\left(\sigma_{2 p_z}^2\right)\right] $

Bonding electrons: 10 (2 from $\sigma_{2 s}$, 6 from $\pi_{2 p}$, and 2 from $\sigma_{2 p_z}$)

Antibonding electrons: 4 (2 from $\sigma_{1 s}^*$ and 2 from $\sigma_{2 s}^*$)

Bond order calculation:

$ \text{Bond Order} = \frac{1}{2}(10-4) = 3 $

Carbon Monoxide (CO):

Total electrons: 6 (from C) + 8 (from O) = 14

$ \left[\left(\sigma_{1 s}^2 \sigma_{1 s}^{*2}\right)\left(\sigma_{2 s}^2 \sigma_{2 s}^{*2}\right)\left(\pi_{2 p_x}^2=\pi_{2 p_y}^2\right)\left(\sigma_{2 p_z}^2\right)\right] $

Bonding electrons: 10 (2 from $\sigma_{2 s}$, 6 from $\pi_{2 p}$, and 2 from $\sigma_{2 p_z}$)

Antibonding electrons: 4 (2 from $\sigma_{1 s}^*$ and 2 from $\sigma_{2 s}^*$)

Bond order calculation:

$ \text{Bond Order} = \frac{1}{2}(10-4) = 3 $

Nitrosyl Ion ($\mathrm{NO}^{+}$):

Total electrons: 7 (from N) + 8 (from O) - 1 (due to positive charge) = 14

$ \left[\left(\sigma_{1 s}^2 \sigma_{1 s}^*{ }^2\right)\left(\sigma_{2 s}^2 \sigma_{2 s}^*\right)\left(\pi_{2 p_x}^2=\pi_{2 p_y}^2\right)\left(\sigma_{2 p_z}^2\right)\right] $

Bonding electrons: 10 (2 from $\sigma_{2 s}$, 6 from $\pi_{2 p}$, and 2 from $\sigma_{2 p_z}$)

Antibonding electrons: 4 (2 from $\sigma_{1 s}^*$ and 2 from $\sigma_{2 s}^*$)

Bond order calculation:

$ \text{Bond Order} = \frac{1}{2}(10-4) = 3 $

Conclusion

All three species, $\mathrm{N}_2$, $\mathrm{CO}$, and $\mathrm{NO}^{+}$, are isoelectronic as they each contain 14 electrons. Moreover, they share an identical bond order of 3.

content: According to the VSEPR theory, what are the most stable shapes of $X_4 F_4$ and $\mathrm{SF}_4$, respectively?
explanation:
$\mathrm{XeF}_4$ : With 4 bonding pairs and 2 lone pairs around xenon, the most stable shape is square planar.

$\mathbf{S F}_4$ : With 4 bonding pairs and 1 lone pair around sulfur, the most stable shape is see-saw.

content: Amongst $\mathrm{O}_2, \mathrm{~N}_2, \mathrm{~F}_2$, and $\mathrm{B}_2$, which molecules will be attracted to an external magnetic field?
explanation:
To determine which molecules are attracted to an external magnetic field, we need to understand their magnetic properties, specifically if they are paramagnetic or diamagnetic. Paramagnetic substances, which have unpaired electrons, are attracted to an external magnetic field. Diamagnetic substances, with all electrons paired, are not attracted to the magnetic field.

$\mathrm{O}_2$ (Oxygen molecule): $\mathrm{O}_2$ is paramagnetic due to the presence of two unpaired electrons in its molecular orbital configuration.

$$ \left(\sigma_{1s}\right)^2\left(\sigma_{1s}^*\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2s}\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2s}^*\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2p_z}\right)^2\left(\pi_{2p_x}\right)^2\left(\pi_{2p_y}\right)^2\left(\pi_{2p_x}^*\right)^1\left(\pi_{2p_y}^*\right)^1 $$

$\mathrm{N}_2$ (Nitrogen molecule): $\mathrm{N}_2$ is diamagnetic because all its electrons are paired in the molecular orbital configuration.

$$ \left(\sigma_{1s}\right)^2\left(\sigma_{1s}^*\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2s}\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2s}^*\right)^2\left(\pi_{2p_x}\right)^2\left(\pi_{2p_y}\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2p_z}\right)^2 $$

$\mathrm{F}_2$ (Fluorine molecule): $\mathrm{F}_2$ is diamagnetic as it has all paired electrons in its molecular orbital configuration.

$$ \left(\sigma_{1s}\right)^2\left(\sigma_{1s}^*\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2s}\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2s}^*\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2p_z}\right)^2\left(\pi_{2p_x}\right)^2\left(\pi_{2p_y}\right)^2\left(\pi_{2p_x}^*\right)^2\left(\pi_{2p_y}^*\right)^2 $$

$B_2$ (Boron molecule): $B_2$ is paramagnetic because it has two unpaired electrons in its molecular orbital configuration.

$$ \left(\sigma_{1s}\right)^2\left(\sigma_{1s}^*\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2s}\right)^2\left(\sigma_{2s}^*\right)^2\left(\pi_{2p_x}\right)^1\left(\pi_{2p_y}\right)^1 $$

Therefore, the molecules that will be attracted to an external magnetic field are $\mathrm{O}_2$ and $\mathrm{B}_2$.

content: Identify the correct order of the molecules with respect to the magnitude of their dipole moment:
explanation:
i) Dipole moment $=0$, (cancelled out)

ii) $\sqrt{P_{C l}^2+P_{C l}^2+2 P_{C l}^2 \cos (120)}=P_{C l}$

iii) $\sqrt{P_{C l}^2+P_{C l}^2+2 P_{C l}^2 \cos (60)}=\sqrt{3} P_{C l}$

iv) $\sqrt{P_F^2+P_F^2+2 P_F^2 \cos (60)}=\sqrt{3} P_F$

$\left|P_F\right|>\left|P_{C l}\right|$ as Fluorine is more electronegative

content: $$ \text { What is the most appropriate Lewis structure of } \mathrm{NO}_2 \mathrm{~F} \text { ? } $$
explanation:
With $\mathrm{NO}_2 \mathrm{~F}$ you'll need to form a double bond between one of the Oxygen atoms and a Nitrogen atom to fill the octets and still use only the 24 valence electrons available for the molecule.

Odd-electron molecules

In molecules with an odd number of electrons like nitric oxide, NO and nitrogen dioxide, $\mathrm{NO}_2$, the octet rule is not satisfied for all the atoms
